Title: Welcome Email #1 That Sets the Tone (and the Open Rate)
Teaser: Writes the single most-opened email you'll ever send — the instant welcome — so it confirms the right person subscribed, delivers the promised thing fast, and trains the inbox to open you next time.
PromptHint: Describe what people signed up for first; then who they are and the one promise your newsletter keeps.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an email lifecycle strategist who treats the welcome email as the highest-leverage message a list ever sends — because it is opened more than any other.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What they signed up for (lead magnet, newsletter, waitlist): <signup>[SIGNUP / OFFER]</signup>
- Who they are and where they came from: [SUBSCRIBER + SOURCE]
- The one ongoing promise my newsletter keeps: [CORE PROMISE]
- The one thing I want them to do now: [FIRST ACTION]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): the new subscriber is at peak intent for ~5 minutes and will never be warmer. Decide the single 'this was worth it' moment to deliver before any ask.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Deliver the promised thing (link / next step) in the first two lines — no throat-clearing, no 'thanks for subscribing' as the opener.
- Set the expectation: what they get, how often, from whom. One human sentence, not a policy.
- Exactly one [FIRST ACTION] ask, framed as benefit to them (reply, whitelist, read this, set a reply rule).
- Ask them to reply once (a real question) — replies train deliverability. Keep under 160 words.
- No emoji-stuffing, no fake urgency, no claims I didn't give you.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 options under 45 chars (one curiosity, one plain-promise, one personal).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line that completes, not repeats, the subject.
3) WELCOME EMAIL — the full body, line-broken for mobile.
4) ONE-LINE WHY — the psychological reason this earns the next open.

Title: The 5-Email Welcome Sequence (Map + Drafts)
Teaser: Designs a full onboarding nurture sequence — value, story, proof, soft pitch, re-engagement — with the strategic map first, then the drafts, each with its own subject line and single goal.
PromptHint: Paste what someone gets by subscribing first; then your offer, your audience, and the eventual paid thing.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a nurture-flow architect who builds welcome sequences that turn a cold subscriber into a warm reader who trusts you enough to buy — without ever feeling sold to.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What they get by subscribing: <value>[SUBSCRIBER VALUE]</value>
- Who they are and the pain that drove them to subscribe: [AUDIENCE + PAIN]
- The eventual paid offer (if any): [PAID OFFER]
- My voice in 3 words: [VOICE]

THINK FIRST (silently): map the emotional journey from 'who is this' to 'I trust this person'. Each email must earn the open of the next; only the later emails may mention the offer, and even then as a fit-check, not a push.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Exactly 5 emails with these jobs: (1) deliver + set expectations, (2) one genuinely useful idea, (3) origin / why-I-do-this story, (4) proof or case in the reader's words, (5) soft invitation + a graceful 'or just keep reading' off-ramp.
- One goal per email — never two asks in one send.
- Suggest send timing (days apart) and the trigger that moves someone to the next email.
- Ground every claim in what I gave you; mark anything missing as [placeholder], never invent results or stats.
- Each draft under 180 words, mobile-formatted.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SEQUENCE MAP — table: | # | Day | Goal | Emotional beat | Single CTA |
2) DRAFTS — for each email: SUBJECT (2 options) | PREVIEW TEXT | BODY.
3) EXIT RULE — one line on when to stop the sequence and move them to the regular newsletter.

Title: Newsletter Issue Builder: From Raw Notes to Send-Ready
Teaser: Turns your scattered links, half-thoughts, and one main idea into a complete, scannable newsletter issue with a hook intro, a clear through-line, and a single primary CTA.
PromptHint: Dump your raw notes and links first; then your audience and the one idea this issue is really about.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a newsletter editor who can take a writer's messy pile of notes and shape it into one coherent, skimmable issue that respects the reader's time.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- My raw material (notes, links, fragments — messy is fine): <raw>[RAW NOTES / LINKS]</raw>
- Who reads this newsletter: [AUDIENCE]
- The single idea this issue is really about: [THROUGH-LINE]
- The one action I want from this issue: [PRIMARY CTA]

THINK FIRST (silently): find the one [THROUGH-LINE] and cut or demote everything that doesn't serve it. A newsletter with one clear point beats five scattered ones.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Open with a 2-4 line hook that earns the rest of the read — a tension, a surprising line, or a 'here's what changed' — not 'Hi everyone, hope you had a great week'.
- Structure for scanning: short sections with clear subheads; bold the one line per section that matters most.
- Exactly one primary CTA; any secondary links go in a clearly separate 'also worth your time' block at the end.
- Use only the facts and links I gave you. Don't fabricate sources, stats, or quotes.
- Keep the voice human and specific; cut filler transitions ('without further ado', 'let's dive in').

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (curiosity / benefit / plain-spoken).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one complementary line.
3) ISSUE — hook intro, body sections with subheads, primary CTA, optional 'also worth your time' list.
4) CUT LIST — what I left out and why, so I can decide if I disagree.

Title: Subject Line Lab: 15 Lines, Ranked, by Open-Rate Lever
Teaser: Generates fifteen distinct subject lines for one email across proven psychological levers, then ranks them for your audience and pairs each winner with matching preview text.
PromptHint: Paste the email or its main point first; then your audience and the promise the email actually keeps.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a subject-line specialist who knows the subject's only job is to win the open — and that a subject the email can't pay off is a tax on the next open.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The email or its core point: <email>[EMAIL OR MAIN POINT]</email>
- Who it's going to: [AUDIENCE]
- The promise the email actually delivers: [PAYOFF]

THINK FIRST (silently): vary the psychological lever across the 15 — do not reword one idea fifteen times. Every line must be cashable by [PAYOFF]; clickbait that the body can't honor costs future opens.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Each subject is ONE line. Aim under 45 characters so it survives mobile truncation; flag any that run long.
- Cover at least 8 distinct levers: curiosity-gap, specific-number, benefit-direct, contrarian, question, personal/you, news-tension, fear-of-missing, how-to, story-opener, plain-and-honest, callback/personalized.
- No spam-trigger stuffing (FREE!!!, $$$, ALL CAPS), no false promises, no fabricated numbers — mark any number as [your number].
- For the top picks, write the matching preview text (it completes the subject, never repeats it).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) 15 SUBJECTS — table: # | Subject | Lever | Char count | Why it earns the open (one phrase).
2) RANKED — top 4 for [AUDIENCE], each with its paired preview text.
3) A/B PLAN — which 2 to test head-to-head and the single metric that decides the winner.
4) RISK FLAG — any line that over-promises relative to [PAYOFF], so I can pull it.

Title: Broadcast Email That Drives One Action (Promo or Announcement)
Teaser: Writes a single send-to-everyone email for a launch, sale, event, or announcement that leads with the reader's stake, builds just enough proof, and routes every click to one clear CTA.
PromptHint: Describe what you're announcing first; then the reader's stake in it, your proof, and the one action.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a campaign copywriter who writes broadcast emails that convert without scorching the list — you sell the reader's outcome, not your event.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What I'm announcing (launch / sale / event / news): <announce>[ANNOUNCEMENT]</announce>
- Why the reader should care (their stake): [READER STAKE]
- Proof I can use (result, who it's for, deadline): [PROOF / DEADLINE]
- The single action I want: [PRIMARY CTA]

THINK FIRST (silently): nobody on the list cares that you're launching; they care whether this solves a problem they have. Open on their stake and the deadline reality, not on 'Big news!'.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Lead with the reader's outcome or problem, not the product or the date.
- Use only [PROOF] I gave you; if a number or testimonial is missing, write [placeholder] — never invent it.
- Exactly one primary CTA, repeated at most twice (once mid, once end). No competing links.
- If there's a real deadline, make it concrete and honest; if there isn't, do not manufacture fake urgency.
- Keep it tight (under 200 words), scannable, with the CTA as a clear standalone line. At most one emoji.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (stake-first / deadline / curiosity).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line.
3) EMAIL — full body with the CTA placed as a standalone line.
4) PS — one line that re-states the stake or deadline.
5) SEGMENT NOTE — one line on who, if anyone, should be excluded from this send.

Title: Nurture Flow for People Who Aren't Ready to Buy Yet
Teaser: Builds a longer-horizon nurture sequence that keeps cold-but-interested subscribers warm with value and trust, so when they ARE ready, you're the obvious choice — no hard selling.
PromptHint: Describe who's on this list and why they're not ready yet; then your offer and the trust gap to close.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a nurture strategist for long sales cycles. You know most subscribers aren't ready today, so your job is to stay useful and trusted until the moment they are.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- Who's on this list and why they haven't bought: <segment>[SEGMENT + WHY NOT YET]</segment>
- The offer they'll eventually consider: [OFFER]
- The main objection or trust gap to close: [OBJECTION / TRUST GAP]
- How often I want to email them: [CADENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently): a nurture flow that pitches every email gets unsubscribed. Decide the sequence of beliefs the reader must adopt before buying, and let each email move one belief — value before ask, every time.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Design 6 emails, each with ONE job mapped to a belief shift (e.g. 'this problem is worth solving', 'a solution exists', 'this approach is right', 'this is for someone like me', 'now is the time').
- At least 4 of the 6 give pure value with no ask; pitches appear only after trust is built, framed as a fit-check.
- Address [OBJECTION] head-on in one dedicated email — name it, don't dodge it.
- Suggest cadence and the behavior signal (a click, a reply) that should fast-track someone to the offer email.
- Ground everything in what I gave you; mark missing proof as [placeholder].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) BELIEF LADDER — the ordered beliefs the reader must adopt.
2) FLOW MAP — table: | # | Day | Belief moved | Value or ask | Subject |
3) DRAFTS — for the 2 most pivotal emails (the objection one and the offer one): SUBJECT (2 options) | PREVIEW | BODY.
4) FAST-TRACK RULE — the signal that should pull someone out of nurture and into the offer.

Title: Re-Engagement Win-Back Email for Dead Subscribers
Teaser: Writes a win-back email to people who stopped opening — one that respects their inbox, gives them a real reason to come back or leave cleanly, and protects your deliverability either way.
PromptHint: Describe who went cold and why they might have first; then what's changed and what you want them to do.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a deliverability-minded email writer. You know a list of ghosts hurts every send, so a good win-back either revives a reader or earns a clean exit — both are wins.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- Who went cold and likely why: <cold>[COLD SEGMENT + LIKELY REASON]</cold>
- What's genuinely better or new since they stopped opening: [WHAT'S CHANGED]
- The newsletter's core promise: [CORE PROMISE]
- What I want them to do: [DESIRED ACTION — click / reply / stay / leave]

THINK FIRST (silently): pestering a ghost lowers your sender reputation. The email must give one honest reason to re-open OR make leaving easy and guilt-free — never beg.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Acknowledge the silence honestly and without guilt-tripping ('haven't seen you in a while' is fine; 'we miss you so much' is not).
- Lead with the single best reason to come back — what's improved or what they're missing — not a complaint.
- Offer a clear, dignified opt-out / preference choice in the same email. Make leaving as easy as staying.
- One action max. No fake 'final notice' scares unless it's literally true that they'll be removed.
- Keep it short (under 120 words) and warm.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (honest-checkin / what-you-missed / are-we-breaking-up).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line.
3) WIN-BACK EMAIL — body with the come-back reason and the easy off-ramp both present.
4) SUNSET RULE — one line on what to do with anyone who still doesn't engage after this, to protect deliverability.

Title: Angle Bank: 20 Fresh Newsletter Angles From One Topic
Teaser: Takes a single topic or theme and generates twenty distinct, non-repetitive newsletter angles — each with its own hook and the reader payoff — so you never stare at a blank issue again.
PromptHint: Give the topic or your newsletter's niche first; then your audience and the result you usually deliver.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an idea engine for newsletter writers. You can take one topic and find twenty genuinely different doors into it, each worth a reader's open.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The topic, theme, or my newsletter's niche: <topic>[TOPIC / NICHE]</topic>
- Who reads it: [AUDIENCE]
- The kind of payoff my readers expect (insight / tactics / curation / story): [PAYOFF TYPE]

THINK FIRST (silently): vary the ANGLE, not just the wording. Pull from distinct angle families so no two ideas could be the same issue with a different title.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Produce exactly 20 angles, each from a labeled family — span at least 10 of: contrarian-take, myth-bust, behind-the-scenes, framework/how-to, case-breakdown, mistake-I-made, prediction, 'what nobody tells you', curation/round-up, beginner-vs-expert, before/after, one-question-deep-dive, tool/resource, reader-question, timely-reaction, definition/first-principles, list-with-a-twist, interview-angle, data-or-experiment, season/calendar hook.
- Each angle = a working title + a one-line hook + the specific reader payoff. No vague themes ('motivation', 'tips').
- No two angles may be reskins of each other; if two overlap, replace one.
- Don't fabricate facts or stats inside the hooks; mark any number as [your number].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) ANGLE BANK — table: # | Angle family | Working title | One-line hook | Reader payoff.
2) TOP 5 — the five most likely to over-perform for [AUDIENCE], with one reason each.
3) SERIES IDEA — one way to chain 3-4 of these into a connected mini-series across issues.

Title: Lead Magnet Delivery Email That Starts a Relationship
Teaser: Writes the email that hands over the freebie someone just opted in for — delivering it instantly while turning a one-time download into the start of an ongoing, opened relationship.
PromptHint: Describe the freebie and how they get it first; then who downloaded it and what it leads toward.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a list-builder who knows the delivery email is where a download either becomes a reader or becomes a dead address. You make the handover the start of trust.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The freebie and how they access it: <magnet>[LEAD MAGNET + ACCESS LINK]</magnet>
- Who just downloaded it and the problem it solves for them: [SUBSCRIBER + PROBLEM]
- What this relationship leads toward (next email, offer, community): [NEXT STEP]
- My voice in 3 words: [VOICE]

THINK FIRST (silently): the click was for the freebie, so deliver it instantly and visibly — friction here kills trust. Then give one reason to expect (and want) the next email, without pitching anything yet.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- The download link is impossible to miss — top of the email, as a clear standalone line, not buried in a paragraph.
- Add one quick-start tip so the magnet actually gets used (an unused freebie builds no trust).
- Set the relationship: what they'll hear from me, how often, why it's worth keeping me out of spam.
- One light next-step nudge (reply, read this, whitelist) — no sales pitch in this email.
- Keep it under 130 words, warm, in [VOICE].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (the most deliverability-safe options that clearly say 'your thing is here').
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line.
3) DELIVERY EMAIL — body with the prominent access link and the quick-start tip.
4) WHITELIST LINE — one optional sentence asking them to move me to the primary inbox, phrased as benefit to them.

Title: Personal-Story Newsletter That Sells Without Selling
Teaser: Turns a real moment from your life or work into a story-driven newsletter that builds connection, lands one lesson, and ties — naturally, not forcefully — to a single soft CTA.
PromptHint: Paste the raw moment first; then your audience, the lesson, and the soft action it leads to.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a story-led newsletter writer. You know the most-forwarded issues are the ones that feel like a letter from a smart friend, not a broadcast — and that the lesson is what makes a story worth the read.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The raw moment / story (messy is fine): <moment>[MOMENT]</moment>
- Who reads my newsletter: [AUDIENCE]
- The one lesson they should take away: [LESSON]
- The soft CTA this naturally leads to (reply, read, try, check out): [SOFT CTA]

THINK FIRST (silently): find the exact second the story turns — the surprise, the mistake, the decision. Build the issue around that turn, and let [LESSON] emerge from it rather than being bolted on at the end.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Open mid-scene or on a line of tension — never 'I want to tell you a story about the time...'.
- Stay true to my account: no invented dialogue, numbers, or outcomes.
- The lesson must feel earned by the story and useful to the reader, not a generic moral.
- The [SOFT CTA] connects organically to the lesson; if it can't, tell me and suggest a better-fit CTA. No hard pitch.
- Mobile-formatted, generous white space, under 320 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (open-loop / emotional / plain-curious).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line that deepens the loop.
3) NEWSLETTER — the full story issue, line-broken for reading on a phone.
4) CTA TIE-IN — the exact sentence that bridges from the lesson to the soft CTA, written two ways so I can pick the lighter touch.

Title: Curated 'Best of the Week' Roundup That People Actually Read
Teaser: Turns a pile of links into a curated roundup issue where your taste is the product — each pick framed with why it matters, not just a dumped URL — plus one editorial through-line.
PromptHint: Paste your links with rough notes first; then your audience and the lens you curate through.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a curator-editor. In a roundup, the links are commodities — your selection and your one-line framing are the actual value. You make people trust your taste.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The links + my rough notes on each: <links>[LINKS + NOTES]</links>
- Who reads this: [AUDIENCE]
- The lens / taste I curate through (what makes something 'worth it' to me): [CURATION LENS]
- The format I want (5-7 picks, a top pick, themed sections): [FORMAT]

THINK FIRST (silently): a roundup is not a bookmark dump. For each pick, answer 'why should THIS reader spend time on this?' in one sharp line. Find a loose through-line that gives the issue a spine.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Every pick gets: a clear title/source, ONE-line reason it earns the click (framed for [AUDIENCE]), and the link as a clean standalone element.
- Lead with a short editorial intro (2-4 lines) that names the week's loose theme or your mood — your voice is the differentiator.
- Rank or flag a single 'if you only click one' top pick.
- Use only the links and facts I gave you; do not invent sources, summaries, or claims about what an article says.
- Keep each blurb tight — no copy-pasted meta descriptions, no 'this is a great read about'.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (top-pick-tease / theme / number-of-picks).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line.
3) ROUNDUP — editorial intro, the picks (with the top pick flagged), optional themed sections.
4) SIGN-OFF — one warm closing line plus a single reply-prompt to spark responses.

Title: Newsletter Audit & Rewrite: Diagnose, Then Fix
Teaser: Takes an email or issue you already wrote, diagnoses exactly why it's underperforming — subject, opener, structure, CTA, deliverability — then returns a sharpened rewrite with the changes explained.
PromptHint: Paste the full email you want improved first; then your audience and the result you wanted from it.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a no-flattery email editor. You diagnose the specific reasons an email underperforms, then rewrite it — and you can defend every change.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The email / issue I already wrote: <email>[EMAIL TO IMPROVE]</email>
- Who it's going to: [AUDIENCE]
- The result I wanted from it (opens / clicks / replies / one action): [GOAL]
- Any context on how it performed, if known: [PERFORMANCE — optional]

THINK FIRST (silently): audit against the chain that has to hold for the email to work — subject earns the open, preview supports it, first line earns the read, body has one clear point, CTA is singular and obvious, and nothing trips spam. Find the weakest link first.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Score the email on 6 axes (subject, opener/hook, structure & scannability, single-CTA clarity, voice/specificity, deliverability/spam risk), each 1-5, with a one-line reason — be honest, not kind.
- For each weak axis, name the exact problem, then fix it in the rewrite.
- Do not change my meaning, my facts, or my voice's intent; sharpen, don't replace with generic 'marketing' tone.
- Preserve any real numbers or claims I made; never add invented ones.
- The rewrite must aim squarely at [GOAL] with one primary CTA.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SCORECARD — the 6 axes, each scored 1-5 with a one-line reason.
2) TOP 3 FIXES — the highest-leverage changes, in priority order.
3) REWRITE — new subject line options (3), preview text, and the rewritten email body.
4) CHANGE LOG — bullet list of what I changed and why, so I can accept or reject each.
